title: On the probability that a binomial variable is at most its expectation
overall_summary: The paper studies q_m = P(Bi(n,m/n) ≤ m), the probability that a binomial random variable with integer expectation m is at most its mean. Its main result proves, for all sufficiently large n, Chvátal's conjecture that q_m is minimized when m is the integer closest to 2n/3, and in fact proves an inverse-unimodality statement for the consecutive differences q_{m+1} − q_m. The core method is a uniform version of Esseen's lattice Edgeworth expansion, including periodic continuity-correction terms, specialized to Bernoulli variables and then combined with Poisson approximation near the endpoints m ≈ 0 and m ≈ n. For the original weighted Bernoulli problem, the paper is directly useful in the equal-weight case and as a guide to the discontinuities and fine lattice effects that occur when the threshold equals the expectation.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Neumann median inequality for binomial variables, quoted as equation (1.1)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let X have the binomial distribution Bi(n,p), and suppose np = m is an integer with m in {0,...,n}. Then P(X < np) < 1/2 < P(X ≤ np). Equivalently, when the mean of a binomial variable is an integer, that mean is a strong median.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper does not reprove this result; it cites Neumann (1966), with other proofs by Jogdeo and Samuels, Kaas and Buhrman, and Knuth. The paper immediately uses it to explain the shape of p ↦ P(Bi(n,p) ≤ np): for fixed n the function oscillates around 1/2, has upward jumps at p = m/n, and is monotone decreasing between consecutive jumps. This observation motivates focusing on the integer-mean values q_m = P(Bi(n,m/n) ≤ m), which are the local maxima of the oscillating function.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1.2, asymptotic Chvátal conjecture and inverse unimodality
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For each n ≥ 2 and each integer m in {0,...,n}, define q_m = P(Bi(n,m/n) ≤ m). There exists n_0 such that for every n ≥ n_0, q_m is smallest when m is the integer closest to 2n/3. Moreover, for n ≥ n_0 and m = 0,...,n−1, the difference q_{m+1} − q_m is negative when m + 1/2 < 2n/3 and positive when m + 1/2 > 2n/3.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Write p_m = m/n. The proof is by estimating q_{m+1} − q_m in several ranges. In the central range 2 log^2 n ≤ m ≤ n − 2 log^2 n, the Bernoulli Edgeworth expansion gives q_m = 1/2 + h_1(p_m)n^{-1/2} + h_3(p_m)n^{-3/2} + O((n p_m(1−p_m))^{-5/2}), where h_1(p) = (2−p)/(3 sqrt(2π p(1−p))) and h_3(p) = (2−p)(p^2+23p−23)/(540 sqrt(2π)(p(1−p))^{3/2}). Differentiating gives h_1'(p) = (3p−2)/(6 sqrt(2π)(p(1−p))^{3/2}), while h_3'(p) = O((p(1−p))^{-5/2}). Subtracting the expansions for p_m and p_{m+1}, and using the mean value theorem, yields q_{m+1} − q_m = h_1'(p'_m)n^{-3/2} + h_3'(p''_m)n^{-5/2} + O((n p_m(1−p_m))^{-5/2}) for some p'_m,p''_m in [p_m,p_{m+1}]. Thus the sign is controlled by 3p_m−2 except in a bounded window around p = 2/3. This immediately gives negativity for 2 log^2 n ≤ m ≤ n/2, negativity for n/2 ≤ m ≤ 2n/3 − C_1 with C_1 large, and positivity for 2n/3 + C_2 ≤ m ≤ n − 2 log^2 n with C_2 large. In the delicate bounded window 2n/3 − C_1 ≤ m ≤ 2n/3 + C_2, the proof uses the Edgeworth expansion two orders further: q_m = 1/2 + h_1(p_m)n^{-1/2} + h_3(p_m)n^{-3/2} + h_5(p_m)n^{-5/2} + O(n^{-7/2}). Taylor expanding at p = 2/3, using h_1'(2/3)=0, gives q_m = G(n) + H(m−2n/3)n^{-5/2} + O(n^{-7/2}), where H(x) = (1/2)h_1''(2/3)x^2 + h_3'(2/3)x. The computed constants are h_1''(2/3) = 27/(8 sqrt π) and h_3'(2/3) = 9/(40 sqrt π) = h_1''(2/3)/15. Therefore q_{m+1} − q_m = [h_1''(2/3)(m−2n/3+1/2) + h_3'(2/3)]n^{-5/2} + O(n^{-7/2}), and the ratio 1/15 < 1/6 gives the required sign on the two sides of the closest integer to 2n/3. For the lower endpoint m < 2 log^2 n, the paper invokes Rigollet and Tong's monotonicity result, whose proof gives q_{m−1} > q_m for m ≤ n/2. For the upper endpoint m > n − 2 log^2 n, the proof uses symmetry and defines q'_m = P(Bi(n,m/n) < m); since 1 − q_m = q'_{n−m}, it suffices to prove q'_{m−1} < q'_m for small m. This is done by total variation Poisson approximation d_TV(Bi(n,m/n),Po(m)) < m/n, the Edgeworth expansion P(Po(m) < m) = 1/2 − (3 sqrt(2π))^{-1}m^{-1/2} − 23(270 sqrt(2π))^{-1}m^{-3/2} + O(m^{-5/2}), and Lemma 6.1 for bounded m.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.1, Esseen lattice Edgeworth expansion
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let X, X_1, X_2,... be independent identically distributed integer-valued random variables of span 1, let S_n = X_1 + ... + X_n, let μ = E X, σ^2 = Var X, and let γ_j be the cumulants of X. Put λ_j = γ_j/σ^j. For j ≥ 1 define polynomials P_j(u) by the formal identity exp(∑_{i≥1} λ_{i+2} u^{i+2} z^i/(i+2)!) = 1 + ∑_{j≥1} P_j(u) z^j. If P_j(u) = ∑_r ϖ_{jr}u^r, define Q_j(x) = ∑_r (−1)^r ϖ_{jr} Φ^{(r)}(x), where Φ is the standard normal distribution function. Define ψ_1(x) = 1/2 − {x}, made right-continuous at integers, and for ℓ ≥ 2 define ψ_ℓ(x) = ∑_{k in Z, k ≠ 0} exp(2πikx)/(2πik)^ℓ. For an integer k ≥ 1, assume E|X|^{k+2} < ∞ and define Ψ_{n,k}(x) = Φ(x) + ∑_{j=1}^k n^{-j/2}Q_j(x) and Ψ^*_{n,k}(x) = Ψ_{n,k}(x) + ∑_{ℓ=1}^k (−1)^{ℓ−1}n^{-ℓ/2}σ^{-ℓ}ψ_ℓ(nμ + xσ sqrt n) Ψ_{n,k}^{(ℓ)}(x). Then P(S_n ≤ nμ + xσ sqrt n) = Ψ^*_{n,k}(x) + R_{n,k}(x), with sup_x |R_{n,k}(x)| = o(n^{-k/2}) as n → ∞. If in addition E|X|^{k+3} < ∞, then sup_x |R_{n,k}(x)| = O(n^{-(k+1)/2}).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This theorem is quoted from Esseen (1945), not reproved in full. The paper notes that the stated O(n^{-(k+1)/2}) version follows immediately by applying Esseen's o(n^{-k/2}) expansion with k replaced by k+1 and then truncating. The terms involving ψ_ℓ are the lattice continuity corrections; the right-continuous convention for ψ_1 is used so that the expansion is valid at lattice points as a right-continuous distribution function.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.2, uniform Edgeworth expansion under an adjacent-mass condition
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Fix an integer k ≥ 1 and a constant c > 0. There exists C, depending only on k and c, such that the following holds. Let X be an integer-valued random variable with E|X|^{k+3} < ∞, mean μ, variance σ^2, and Λ_j = E|X−μ|^j/σ^j. Assume min{P(X=0), P(X=1)} ≥ cσ^2. Let n ≥ 2 be an integer satisfying σ sqrt n ≥ log n. For S_n = X_1 + ... + X_n with X_i independent copies of X, the Esseen expansion P(S_n ≤ nμ + xσ sqrt n) = Ψ^*_{n,k}(x) + R_{n,k}(x), with Ψ^*_{n,k} as in Theorem 3.1, holds uniformly with sup_x |R_{n,k}(x)| ≤ C Λ_{k+3} n^{-(k+1)/2} ≤ C E|X|^{k+3} σ^{-(k+3)} n^{-(k+1)/2}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof follows Esseen's Fourier-analytic proof but tracks constants uniformly over the distribution of X. Define f(t)=E exp(it(X−μ)), f_n(t)=f(t/(σ sqrt n))^n, and g_0(t)=exp(−t^2/2)+∑_{j=1}^k n^{-j/2}P_j(it)exp(−t^2/2). Set T = n^{(k+1)/2} and T_1 = sqrt n / Λ_{k+3}^{1/(k+1)}. If Λ_{k+3}n^{-(k+1)/2} is not small, the desired estimate is trivial using Lemma 4.1, so the proof assumes it is small. For |t| ≤ T_1, Petrov's version of the characteristic-function Edgeworth estimate gives |f_n(t) − g_0(t)| ≤ CΛ_{k+3}|t|^{k+3}exp(−t^2/13)n^{-(k+1)/2}, hence ∫_{−T_1}^{T_1}|f_n(t)−g_0(t)|/|t| dt ≤ CΛ_{k+3}n^{-(k+1)/2}. For larger |t| up to πσ sqrt n, Lemma 4.2 and the adjacent-mass assumption give |f_n(t)| ≤ exp(−ct^2), so the tails ∫_{T_1}^{πσ sqrt n}|f_n(t)|/|t| dt and the corresponding integral for g_0 are bounded by C exp(−cT_1^2), which is at most CΛ_{k+3}n^{-(k+1)/2}. The same argument without the factor 1/|t| gives ∫_{−πσ sqrt n}^{πσ sqrt n}|f_n(t)−g_0(t)| dt ≤ CΛ_{k+3}n^{-(k+1)/2}. Esseen's lattice inversion then produces additional shifted-denominator terms with denominators |t+2πjσ sqrt n|. These are bounded by C(|j|σ sqrt n)^{-1}Λ_{k+3}n^{-(k+1)/2}; summing over the relevant j contributes a factor log n/(σ sqrt n), which is absorbed exactly by the hypothesis σ sqrt n ≥ log n. The remaining parts are handled as in Esseen's proof.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Remark 3.4, reduced continuity-correction expansion
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the setting of Theorem 3.1 or Theorem 3.2, the expansion may be written with redundant higher-order products removed. Define tilde Ψ^*_{n,k}(x) = Ψ_{n,k}(x) + ∑_{ℓ=1}^k (−1)^{ℓ−1}n^{-ℓ/2}σ^{-ℓ}ψ_ℓ(nμ + xσ sqrt n) Ψ_{n,k−ℓ}^{(ℓ)}(x). Then P(S_n ≤ nμ + xσ sqrt n) = tilde Ψ^*_{n,k}(x) + tilde R_{n,k}(x), and the same remainder bounds as in Theorem 3.1 and Theorem 3.2 hold.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The full Ψ^*_{n,k} contains terms whose total order is beyond n^{-k/2}. Removing them changes the expansion only by terms of order O(n^{-m/2}) with m ≥ k+1. For Theorem 3.2, Lemma 4.1 bounds the removed terms by the same quantity Λ_{k+3}n^{-(k+1)/2}, using also the lower bound implicit in σ sqrt n ≥ log n and the fact that for integer-valued X, lower moments are controlled by higher moments. This reduced form is used in Section 5 to evaluate the expansion at x=0.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.1, uniform bounds for Edgeworth coefficients and derivatives
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let ℓ ≥ 1 and suppose E|X|^{ℓ+2} < ∞. With Λ_j = E|X−μ|^j/σ^j and with P_ℓ(u), Q_ℓ(x), and Ψ_{n,ℓ}(x) defined as in the Edgeworth expansion, for every derivative order m ≥ 0 there are constants C_ℓ and C_{ℓ,m} such that |ϖ_{ℓ,r}| ≤ C_ℓΛ_{ℓ+2}, |P_ℓ(u)| ≤ C_ℓΛ_{ℓ+2}(|u|^{ℓ+2}+|u|^{3ℓ}), |Q_ℓ^{(m)}(x)| ≤ C_{ℓ,m}Λ_{ℓ+2}, and |Ψ_{n,ℓ}^{(m)}(x)| ≤ C_{ℓ,m}(1+Λ_{ℓ+2}n^{-ℓ/2}) for all relevant r,u,x,n.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: From the formal definition of P_ℓ, each coefficient ϖ_{ℓ,r} is a linear combination of products ∏_{s=1}^a λ_{i_s+2} with ∑_s i_s = ℓ and ∑_s(i_s+2)=r. Cumulants satisfy |λ_j| ≤ C_jΛ_j, and Hölder's inequality gives Λ_{i_s+2} ≤ Λ_{ℓ+2}^{i_s/ℓ}. Thus each product is at most C_ℓΛ_{ℓ+2}, proving the coefficient bound. The polynomial bound for P_ℓ follows from the degree range j+2 ≤ r ≤ 3j. The derivative bound for Q_ℓ follows because Φ and all its derivatives are bounded on R. Finally Ψ_{n,ℓ}^{(m)} is Φ^{(m)} plus a finite sum n^{-j/2}Q_j^{(m)}; applying the previous bounds and Hölder again gives the displayed estimate.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.2, characteristic-function damping from two adjacent atoms
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: If X is a random variable satisfying P(X=0) ≥ a and P(X=1) ≥ a for some a ≥ 0, then for all |t| ≤ π, |E exp(itX)| ≤ 1 − aπ^{-2}t^2 ≤ exp(−aπ^{-2}t^2).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Subtract the masses at 0 and 1: E exp(itX) − a − a exp(it) is the Fourier transform of a positive measure of total mass 1−2a. Therefore |E exp(itX)| ≤ a|1+exp(it)| + 1−2a = 2a|cos(t/2)| + 1−2a. For |t| ≤ π this equals 1 − 2a(1−cos(t/2)) = 1 − 4a sin^2(t/4), and the elementary lower bound on sin on [0,π/4] gives 1 − aπ^{-2}t^2. The exponential bound follows from 1−y ≤ exp(−y).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Equations (5.1) and (5.2), Edgeworth expansion evaluated at the mean
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the setting of the reduced expansion, taking x=0 gives P(S_n ≤ nμ) = Ψ_{n,k}(0) + ∑_{ℓ=1}^k (−1)^{ℓ−1}n^{-ℓ/2}σ^{-ℓ}ψ_ℓ(nμ) Ψ_{n,k−ℓ}^{(ℓ)}(0) + tilde R_{n,k}(0). If additionally nμ is an integer, ψ_ℓ(nμ)=ψ_ℓ(0)=−B_ℓ/ℓ!, where B_ℓ are Bernoulli numbers, and with Q_0=Φ this becomes P(S_n ≤ nμ) = 1/2 + ∑_{s=1}^k n^{-s/2} ∑_{ℓ=0}^s σ^{-ℓ}(−1)^ℓ B_ℓ Q_{s−ℓ}^{(ℓ)}(0)/ℓ! + tilde R_{n,k}(0). All terms with even s vanish.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The first formula is just the reduced Edgeworth expansion at x=0. When nμ is an integer, the periodic functions ψ_ℓ are evaluated at 0; the paper uses ψ_ℓ(0)=−B_ℓ/ℓ!, with B_1=−1/2, B_2=1/6, and B_{2j+1}=0 for j ≥ 1. Reindexing the double sum by total order s=j+ℓ gives the displayed expansion. The parity cancellation follows because φ is even, so its odd derivatives at 0 vanish, and because the coefficients ϖ_{jr} in Q_j satisfy ϖ_{jr}=0 unless r−j is even. Therefore Q_j^{(ℓ)}(0)=0 whenever j and ℓ have the same parity, except for j=ℓ=0; this kills every even total order s. Remark 5.1 adds that for P(S_n < nμ), the same formulas hold except that the sign is changed for the terms with ℓ=1, because ψ_1 must be taken left-continuously.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Remark 1.4 and equation (1.2), first-order expansion for arbitrary p
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For general p, at least under the condition n p(1−p) ≥ log^2 n, the paper states the asymptotic formula P(Bi(n,p) ≤ np) = 1/2 + [4 − 2p − 6{np}]/[6 sqrt(2π n p(1−p))] + O(1/(n p(1−p))). Here {np} denotes the fractional part of np.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is stated as a consequence of Theorem 3.2 and equation (5.1) with k=1. For X distributed as Bernoulli(p), the normalized third cumulant contributes the ordinary first Edgeworth correction Q_1(0), and the lattice correction contributes a term involving ψ_1(np)=1/2−{np}. Combining these two first-order terms gives the numerator 4−2p−6{np}. The theorem's uniform error bound gives the stated O(1/(n p(1−p))) remainder under n p(1−p) ≥ log^2 n.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Equations (5.6) to (5.8), binomial expansion at an integer mean through order n^{-3/2}
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let S_n have the binomial distribution Bi(n,p), with 0 < p < 1, and suppose np is an integer. If sqrt(n p(1−p)) ≥ log n, then P(S_n ≤ np) = 1/2 + h_1(p)n^{-1/2} + h_3(p)n^{-3/2} + O((n p(1−p))^{-5/2}), where h_1(p) = (2−p)/(3 sqrt(2π p(1−p))) and h_3(p) = (2−p)(p^2+23p−23)/(540 sqrt(2π)(p(1−p))^{3/2}). The terms of orders n^{-1} and n^{-2} vanish.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For X distributed as Bernoulli(p), σ^2=p(1−p) and all moments exist. Also E|X−p|^j ≤ σ^2, so Λ_j ≤ σ^{2−j}, and min{P(X=0),P(X=1)} ≥ σ^2; hence Theorem 3.2 applies with c=1 whenever sqrt(n p(1−p)) ≥ log n and gives remainders O((n p(1−p))^{-(k+1)/2}). The integer-mean formula (5.2) is then applied with k=4. Computing the Bernoulli cumulants γ_3, γ_4, and γ_5 and substituting them into Q_1, Q_2, and Q_3 yields the displayed h_1 and h_3. The absence of h_2 and h_4 is exactly the parity cancellation in the integer-mean Edgeworth expansion.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Derivative estimates used in Section 6
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For h_1 and h_3 defined in the binomial integer-mean expansion, h_1'(p) = (3p−2)/(6 sqrt(2π)(p(1−p))^{3/2}), so h_1 decreases on (0,2/3), increases on (2/3,1), and has its unique critical point at p=2/3. Also h_3'(p)=O((p(1−p))^{-5/2}). At p=2/3 the higher constants used in the critical window are h_1''(2/3)=27/(8 sqrt π) and h_3'(2/3)=9/(40 sqrt π)=h_1''(2/3)/15.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The formula for h_1' follows by differentiating h_1(p)=(2−p)/(3 sqrt(2π p(1−p))). The bound on h_3' follows directly from the explicit rational expression for h_3(p), since differentiating increases the endpoint singularity by one power of p(1−p). In the bounded critical window near 2/3, the paper differentiates explicitly again to obtain h_1''(2/3) and h_3'(2/3). These constants determine the sign of q_{m+1}−q_m when the leading derivative h_1' is of order 1/n.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 6.1, monotonicity of Poisson probabilities at the mean
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For every integer m ≥ 0, P(Po(m) < m) < P(Po(m+1) < m+1) < 1/2, and P(Po(m) ≤ m) > P(Po(m+1) ≤ m+1) > 1/2.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Consider a Poisson process of rate 1 on (0,∞), and let T_1<T_2<... be its arrival times. The number of points in [0,m] has distribution Po(m), so P(Po(m)<m)=P(T_m>m)=∫_m^∞ t^{m−1}e^{−t}/(m−1)! dt. Subtracting the analogous formula for m+1 gives P(Po(m)<m) − P(Po(m+1)<m+1) = ∫_m^{m+1}[t^m e^{−t} − m^m e^{−m}]/m! dt, which is negative because t^m e^{−t} decreases for t ≥ m. Similarly, P(Po(m)≤m) − P(Po(m+1)≤m+1) = ∫_m^{m+1}[t^{m+1}e^{−t} − m^{m+1}e^{−m}]/(m+1)! dt, which is positive because t^{m+1}e^{−t} increases for t ≤ m+1. The inequalities relative to 1/2 follow from these monotonicities together with the normal approximation limit 1/2 as m → ∞.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Endpoint Poisson approximation estimates, equations (6.14) to (6.16)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For q'_m = P(Bi(n,m/n) < m), the paper uses the standard total variation bound d_TV(Bi(n,p),Po(np)) < p to get |q'_m − P(Po(m)<m)| < m/n. It also uses the expansion P(Po(m)<m) = 1/2 − [1/(3 sqrt(2π))]m^{-1/2} − [23/(270 sqrt(2π))]m^{-3/2} + O(m^{-5/2}). Consequently, for m ≥ 2, q'_m − q'_{m−1} = [1/(6 sqrt(2π))]m^{-3/2} + O(m^{-5/2}) + O(m/n), which is positive for C_3 < m < 2 log^2 n and n sufficiently large, with C_3 fixed large enough.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The total variation estimate is cited from Barbour, Holst, and Janson's Poisson approximation theorem. The Poisson expansion is obtained by applying Theorem 3.2, or the classical Esseen theorem, to a sum of m independent Poisson(1) variables and using the strict-inequality version from Remark 5.1, which changes the sign of the ℓ=1 continuity-correction terms. Subtracting consecutive values of the expansion gives the m^{-3/2} leading difference. The approximation error contributes O(m/n), which is negligible in the endpoint range m < 2 log^2 n after C_3 is fixed and n is large. For bounded m ≤ C_3, the strict monotonicity is supplied directly by Lemma 6.1 and the same total variation approximation.

proof_techniques.name: Lattice Edgeworth expansion with periodic continuity corrections
proof_techniques.description: The paper uses Esseen's expansion for integer-valued sums, not just the continuous Edgeworth expansion. The correction terms involve periodic functions ψ_ℓ(nμ + xσ sqrt n), and these terms encode the discontinuities and fractional-part effects of a lattice distribution. The convention ψ_1(x)=1/2−{x}, right-continuously at integers, is important because the distribution function is right-continuous and the expansion is evaluated exactly at lattice points.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original problem contains exact threshold events for Bernoulli sums, where lattice jumps are often decisive. In the equal-weight case, this technique directly analyzes P(Bi(m,p) ≥ mp) or its complementary lower-tail forms at and near the expectation.

proof_techniques.name: Uniformizing Edgeworth estimates by controlling the characteristic function away from zero
proof_techniques.description: The paper proves a uniform version of Esseen's expansion by requiring a fixed lower bound, relative to variance, on P(X=0) and P(X=1). Lemma 4.2 then gives a Gaussian-type decay bound |E exp(itX)| ≤ exp(−ct^2) for |t|≤π after normalization, preventing the lattice span from degenerating and allowing the Fourier inversion errors to be controlled uniformly.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Bernoulli variables and many Bernoulli-derived lattice variables have adjacent atoms, so this mechanism can be reused to obtain uniform asymptotics across ranges of p. For more general weighted sums, an analogue would be needed to rule out near-periodic or coarse-lattice obstructions.

proof_techniques.name: Evaluation at an integer mean using Bernoulli numbers
proof_techniques.description: At x=0 and nμ integer, all periodic corrections are evaluated at zero, where ψ_ℓ(0)=−B_ℓ/ℓ!. This transforms the Edgeworth expansion into an explicit algebraic series involving derivatives Q_j^{(ℓ)}(0). Because the normal density is even and the Edgeworth polynomials have a parity constraint, all even total orders vanish in the integer-mean expansion.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: When a weighted Bernoulli sum has a threshold exactly at its mean and a lattice structure, this kind of simplification can identify the first nonzero correction to 1/2. It is especially relevant for equal weights or rational weights after clearing denominators.

proof_techniques.name: Finite-difference sign from the derivative of the leading correction
proof_techniques.description: For q_m=P(Bi(n,m/n)≤m), the leading correction is h_1(m/n)n^{-1/2}. Since consecutive m values change p by 1/n, q_{m+1}−q_m is controlled to first order by h_1'(m/n)n^{-3/2}. The sign change h_1'(p) ∝ 3p−2 is the main reason the minimum occurs near p=2/3.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For identifying exceptional p-values in the original problem, one can look for where the leading asymptotic correction changes sign in the equal-weight model. This gives candidate thresholds or worst cases before attempting a general weighted proof.

proof_techniques.name: Higher-order expansion in a critical window
proof_techniques.description: Where the derivative of the leading correction vanishes, the paper expands to two more Edgeworth orders and then Taylor-expands around the critical point p=2/3. This reduces q_m near the minimum to G(n)+H(m−2n/3)n^{-5/2}+O(n^{-7/2}), with an explicit quadratic-linear polynomial H. The exact constants h_1''(2/3) and h_3'(2/3) then decide the sign of q_{m+1}−q_m at the integer scale.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the original problem has borderline p values where a first-order asymptotic is inconclusive, this illustrates how to resolve the lattice-scale question by keeping additional Edgeworth terms rather than relying only on the central limit theorem.

proof_techniques.name: Endpoint reduction to Poisson approximation
proof_techniques.description: The uniform normal/Edgeworth expansion used in the central range requires n p(1−p) large. Near p=0 or p=1, the paper switches to Poisson approximation: Bi(n,m/n) is close in total variation to Po(m) with error less than m/n. Monotonicity and a separate Poisson Edgeworth expansion then control the endpoint consecutive differences.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For weighted Bernoulli sums with very small or very large p, Poisson or compound-Poisson approximations may replace normal approximations. This is a useful way to handle sparse regimes that are invisible to a variance-large analysis.

proof_techniques.name: Symmetry between lower and upper binomial tails
proof_techniques.description: The paper repeatedly uses the identity that n−Bi(n,p) has distribution Bi(n,1−p). In Theorem 1.2, this converts the upper endpoint m≈n for q_m into a small-parameter problem for strict lower tails q'_m=P(Bi(n,m/n)<m). Remark 1.3 also notes that the maximizer of P(Bi(n,m/n)<m) is near n/3 by symmetry from the minimizer near 2n/3.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original event is an upper-tail event, while the paper mainly studies lower tails. Complementing Bernoulli variables converts weighted upper-tail questions into lower-tail questions for success probability 1−p, with a corresponding change in the threshold.

proof_techniques.name: Strong-median and oscillation viewpoint
proof_techniques.description: The quoted Neumann theorem shows that an integer binomial mean is a strict median: the lower strict tail is below 1/2 and the lower weak tail is above 1/2. Consequently, p ↦ P(Bi(n,p)≤np) does not vary smoothly around 1/2 but instead decreases between jumps and jumps upward at rational points m/n.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This warns that proving inequalities at the expectation cannot ignore whether the threshold is attained as a lattice point. In equal-weight Bernoulli sums, the distinction between ≥, >, ≤, and < at mp integer can change the extremal behavior.

other_useful_info: Conjecture 1.1, due to Chvátal, asserts that for every fixed n ≥ 2, q_m=P(Bi(n,m/n)≤m) is minimized at the integer m closest to 2n/3; the paper proves this only for sufficiently large n and reports numerical verification for n ≤ 1000. The paper also conjectures that the inverse-unimodality statement for q_m should hold for all n, while noting that Rigollet and Tong (2011) proved for every n that q_m decreases for m ≤ n/2, and their proof gives strict decrease in the range used here. Remark 1.3 states that, by symmetry, for large n the probability P(Bi(n,m/n)<m) is largest when m is the integer closest to n/3. Remark 1.5 says the constants in the proof could in principle be made explicit to obtain an explicit n_0, but the author does not regard that as practical. The paper points to explicit related binomial-tail bounds by Doerr (2018), Greenberg and Mohri (2014), Pelekis and Ramon (2016), Rigollet and Tong (2011), and Slud (1977), and to Brown, Cai, and DasGupta (2002) for another use of multi-term Edgeworth expansions in binomial oscillation phenomena.
why_relevant: Analyzes binomial distribution functions at the mean and formulates the Chvátal-Janson conjecture about the case p=m/n. This is directly relevant to the discontinuities that occur in the equal-weight version of the problem.
